does: This paper performs a meta-research survey integrating four previously disconnected research tracks—diffusion-based adversarial attacks on text and LLMs, attacks on image classifiers, jailbreaks for vision-language models, and diffusion-based defenses—into a unified conceptual framework. The authors catalog approximately fifty published papers across these domains, develop a unified taxonomy of how diffusion models function in adversarial pipelines, and establish common evaluation criteria and threat models. They focus particularly on the LLM-victim slice and propose a six-class taxonomy that characterizes diffusion's various roles in adversarial attack and defense strategies, complemented by a threat-model axis for systematic classification.
connects: This work responds to the fragmentation that has emerged as adversarial machine learning has specialized into separate communities studying text, vision, and multimodal systems. It builds on foundational adversarial robustness research while recognizing that denoising diffusion models have become a powerful generative mechanism for both attacks and defenses across these modalities. The survey synthesizes insights from adversarial text generation, image perturbation techniques, vision-language model vulnerabilities, and input purification methods—each of which developed independently with distinct vocabularies and benchmarking practices. By identifying diffusion as the shared underlying mechanism, it bridges technical approaches that were previously treated as orthogonal lines of investigation.
enables: This integrative framework enables future research to leverage cross-modal insights when designing adversarial attacks and defenses. By establishing unified evaluation criteria and shared threat models, the taxonomy makes it easier to transfer techniques between domains and to compare new methods against a common set of baselines. The six-class taxonomy of diffusion roles provides a conceptual scaffold for understanding and inventing novel adversarial pipelines that exploit or defend against diffusion-based mechanisms. This foundation supports more efficient development of robust AI systems and creates a common language for adversarial researchers across text, vision, and vision-language communities to collaborate and validate results.
